name: Platform-Neutral Authentication Architecture
consequences: Adopting open standards like FIDO2 reduces vendor lock-in but requires reengineering trust models currently tied to platform-specific signals. This could delay deployment if existing public-sector systems lack compatibility with non-proprietary authentication flows.
options: Adopt Open-Source FIDO2 Implementations
Leverage Hardware Tokens with OS-Neutral APIs
Develop a Browser-Based Fallback Protocol
review: Controls technical standardization vs. vendor flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory approval timelines for open-source solutions.

name: AltID Procurement Influence Strategy
consequences: Embedding platform-neutrality requirements into AltID specifications could force systemic changes but risks pushing stakeholders toward temporary workarounds. Early engagement may create dependency on project-defined standards.
options: Mandate Multi-Platform Certification Criteria
Create a Public-Private Standards Advisory Panel
Offer Incentivized Pilot Programs for Non-Duopoly Solutions
review: Controls direct procurement intervention vs. indirect standards pressure. Weakness: The options fail to account for potential legal challenges from incumbent platform providers.

name: Coalition-Driven Policy Framing
consequences: Building a multi-stakeholder coalition strengthens advocacy but dilutes focus on technical priorities. Over-reliance on civil society partners may weaken credibility with institutional decision-makers.
options: Form an Industry-Academia Regulatory Task Force
Leverage EU Digital Identity Wallet Working Groups
Establish a Cross-Sector Continuity-of-Service Advisory Board
review: Controls ideological alignment vs. pragmatic compromise. Weakness: The options fail to address potential conflicts between privacy advocates and public-sector continuity requirements.

name: Demonstrator-Driven Technical Proof
consequences: Creating non-production demonstrators validates feasibility but may be dismissed as 'academic' without institutional backing. Over-specifying requirements risks misalignment with actual public-sector constraints.
options: Build a Minimal Viable Fallback Authentication Stack
Simulate Platform-Neutral Trust Scenarios in Isolated Environments
Develop a Modular Authentication Plugin Architecture
review: Controls technical rigor vs. political viability. Weakness: The options fail to consider the risk of demonstrators being perceived as competitive threats to existing systems.

name: Regulatory Risk Mitigation Framework
consequences: Proactively addressing procurement compliance reduces legal exposure but requires navigating complex regulatory landscapes. Over-optimization for current standards may limit future flexibility.
options: Create a Dual-Track Certification Pathway
Implement a Dynamic Risk Assessment Dashboard
Establish a Pre-Approval Audit Trail for Alternative Solutions
review: Controls proactive compliance vs. reactive adaptation. Weakness: The options fail to account for potential contradictions with existing eIDAS framework interpretations.

name: Supplier Diversification Mandate
consequences: Imposing procurement requirements for multi-vendor authentication solutions would increase competition among identity providers. However, this risks delaying implementation as existing platform contracts may lack exit clauses or interoperability provisions.
options: Mandate multi-vendor authentication contracts for public-sector systems
Incentivize local provider participation through grant-based certification
Establish a centralized certification body for alternative authentication methods
review: Controls mandatory supplier diversification vs. maintaining existing vendor relationships. Weakness: The options fail to consider contractual obligations with current providers.

name: Regulatory Resilience Framing
consequences: Reframing digital identity as a critical infrastructure risk would prioritize continuity over convenience. This could pressure policymakers to adopt stricter procurement standards but might face resistance from stakeholders prioritizing user experience.
options: Embed resilience metrics in national cybersecurity regulations
Leverage EU Digital Identity Wallet frameworks to standardize fallback requirements
Create public-private partnerships for contingency planning
review: Controls risk-based regulation vs. user-centric design. Weakness: The options neglect the technical complexity of integrating fallback mechanisms into existing systems.

name: Decentralized Authentication Pilot
consequences: Testing blockchain-based or federated identity models could demonstrate viable alternatives to platform-centric systems. However, these approaches may struggle with adoption due to regulatory uncertainty and technical immaturity.
options: Deploy blockchain-enabled identity verification for low-risk public services
Implement federated identity architectures with cross-platform compatibility
Develop open-source community-driven authentication protocols
review: Controls experimental decentralization vs. incremental platform integration. Weakness: The options overlook the political challenges of displacing established vendor ecosystems.

name: Procurement Transparency Initiative
consequences: Publicly disclosing supplier dependency metrics would increase accountability but could provoke backlash from platform providers or entrenched vendors. This might accelerate reform but also trigger defensive lobbying.
options: Require annual public reporting of authentication system vendor concentrations
Implement third-party audits of platform dependency risks
Create real-time monitoring dashboards for procurement decisions
review: Controls transparency mandates vs. vendor confidentiality. Weakness: The options fail to address how to handle sensitive commercial agreements.

name: Cross-Platform Interoperability Standards
consequences: Developing open authentication standards would reduce lock-in but might face resistance from platforms prioritizing closed ecosystems. Success depends on aligning with broader EU digital identity initiatives.
options: Propose open standards for platform-agnostic authentication protocols
Engage EU technical working groups to prioritize interoperability
Form industry coalitions to jointly develop open-source authentication frameworks
review: Controls open standards adoption vs. platform-specific solutions. Weakness: The options neglect the resource requirements for sustained standardization efforts.

name: Alternative Authentication Method Development
consequences: Shifting focus to non-platform-specific authentication methods reduces reliance on Apple/Google but risks institutional resistance due to existing integration complexities. This could delay adoption if stakeholders prioritize familiarity over long-term resilience.
options: Develop FIDO2-based fallback mechanisms using open-source tools
Implement hardware token integration with existing Danish infrastructure
Explore browser-based authentication without OS dependencies
review: Controls technical innovation vs. institutional inertia. Weakness: The options fail to consider the regulatory approval timelines for new authentication methods.

name: Open Standards Advocacy
consequences: Promoting open authentication protocols strengthens long-term interoperability but may face opposition from platform vendors. This could slow progress if EU or national bodies prioritize proprietary solutions for political or economic reasons.
options: Push for eIDAS2 compliance with open authentication protocols
Lobby EU bodies to mandate platform-neutral authentication in digital identity frameworks
Develop open-source standards for fallback authentication paths
review: Controls EU alignment vs. domestic policy. Weakness: The options fail to address potential technical incompatibilities with existing systems.

name: Stakeholder Coalition Expansion
consequences: Broadening coalition support increases pressure on policymakers but risks diluting focus if new members prioritize conflicting interests. This could weaken the project's ability to secure targeted procurement changes.
options: Engage civil society organizations in public awareness campaigns
Form alliances with international privacy-focused tech groups
Incorporate academic researchers into policy drafting processes
review: Controls broad engagement vs. focused advocacy. Weakness: The options fail to account for resource allocation challenges in maintaining diverse stakeholder relationships.

name: Regulatory Risk Repositioning
consequences: Reframing dependency risks as procurement compliance issues may streamline institutional buy-in but could limit the scope of technical solutions if regulators prioritize cost over resilience. This might create unintended constraints on innovation.
options: Frame platform neutrality as a compliance requirement for public contracts
Link fallback authentication to national cybersecurity standards
Position supplier concentration as a threat to public service continuity
review: Controls compliance focus vs. technical flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to address potential legal ambiguities in redefining risk categories.

name: Technical Demonstration Optimization
consequences: Refining demonstrators to emphasize resilience over novelty strengthens credibility but may reduce visibility if stakeholders prioritize immediate usability. This could limit the project's ability to attract broader attention.
options: Prioritize fallback path demonstrations over feature-rich prototypes
Use GrapheneOS as a baseline for privacy-respecting authentication tests
Integrate hardware tokens into existing public-sector test environments
review: Controls technical focus vs. stakeholder appeal. Weakness: The options fail to consider the resource intensity of maintaining specialized demonstration environments.
